event: Saints Cosmas and Damian treating a chest wound
symbols: hands tending the wound — Christ-like healing and compassion for the suffering
bare chest of patient — vulnerability and trust in the healers
red cloth on table — instruments of healing and echo of blood and sacrifice
two healers working together — fraternal cooperation in service of the suffering
description: Two bearded men tend to a third man lying on a bed or table with his chest exposed, showing a wound. One healer leans over the patient from the right, carefully dressing or examining the wound, while the other assists from the left with a gesture of prayer or support. The patient's head is tilted back. A red cloth with surgical instruments sits on a surface nearby. The scene is warmly lit against a dark background, with the flesh tones of the three men glowing in the light.
theological_description: Saints Cosmas and Damian, the anargyroi (silverless ones) of early Christian tradition, practiced medicine without accepting payment, embodying Christ's command to 'heal the sick' freely (Matthew 10:8). Their charitable healing ministry reflects the theology that physical healing is an expression of divine compassion. Jesus Himself healed the sick throughout His ministry as a sign of the Kingdom of God's inbreaking (Luke 4:18-19), and He sent His disciples to do the same. The hands of these physician-saints become instruments of grace, recalling the Good Samaritan who bound up the wounds of the injured man, pouring on oil and wine (Luke 10:34). Their selfless service embodies the apostolic teaching that each believer's gifts — including the gift of healing (1 Corinthians 12:9) — are to be exercised for the benefit of others, not for personal gain. The intimate tenderness of the scene reflects the very heart of the gospel: God drawing near to human suffering with compassion and power to restore.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ's command to heal the sick freely — the direct model for these physician-saints who accepted no payment
The Good Samaritan binding wounds parallels the saints' hands-on care of the injured man
The gift of healing given by the Spirit, exercised by these saints for the common good
Christ's mission to heal the brokenhearted, which these saints continue in His name

_meta.title: Saint Cosmas and Saint Damian Dressing a Chest Wound
